schools.summary: Moses' objection of unbelief — 'they will not believe me... The LORD hath not appeared unto thee' (anticipating Israel's rejection); the word 'behold' expressing not 'perhaps' but a positive conviction, his faith weak (Ellicott); the reluctance judged — too much cowardice, slothfulness, and unbelief, though partly humility (Henry), or more gently, obeying hesitatingly not from stubbornness but timidity, shrinking under the task's greatness (Calvin); Moses remembering Israel once rejected him (Ex 2:14) and fearing it again unless he showed a sign (Wesley).

themes: The LORD hath not appeared unto thee

schools.summary: The question and the shepherd's rod — 'What is that in thine hand? A rod' (matteh, his shepherd's crook, the ordinary staff of a herdsman, Clarke/Ellicott); God answering Moses' fear not with reproof but a sign, granting the means to win Israel's belief (Gill); the choice of so mean an instrument significant — God working his wonders not with sword or sceptre but a shepherd's rod, that the power be plainly his; the sign having reference to Moses' calling, the shepherd of sheep to become shepherd of God's people (Keil and Delitzsch); later named 'the rod of God' (Ex 4:20).

themes: What is that in thine hand?

schools.summary: The rod turned to a serpent — 'cast it on the ground... and it became a serpent' (the lifeless staff transformed into a living snake at God's word); 'Moses fled from before it' (starting back in fear, evidence it was a real and dangerous serpent, not a mere appearance; nachash a generic term telling nothing of the species, Gen 3:1, Clarke/Ellicott, but his flight showing it no harmless thing); the suddenness and reality demonstrating God's power over the nature of things, dead wood become a living fearful creature; Moses startled, the wonder God's not his own contrivance.

The serpent restored and Moses' faith — 'take it by the tail' (a trial of courage; not by the neck, the safe way men handle dangerous snakes, but the more perilous hold, Ellicott; God bidding the very thing that would seem to expose him to the strike); the faith required — having just fled the creature, it took considerable faith to seize it as commanded, yet he obeyed (Clarke); 'it became a rod in his hand' (the living serpent instantly the lifeless staff again); partly to assure Moses it was a true serpent, no illusion (Gill); God's mastery over creation in both directions, training Moses in the obedience of faith.

themes: it became a rod in his hand

schools.summary: The purpose of the sign — an elliptical sentence, supplied 'Do this before them, that they may believe...' (Clarke, Wesley); God's words continuing, Moses' action (v4) parenthetic (Ellicott); the object — that the God of their fathers, the covenant God, had truly appeared to Moses and sent him, the sign authenticating his commission; the power given to be repeated as needed, to win Israel's credit and repress Pharaoh (Calvin); the miracle not for Moses' own conviction (confirmed at the bush) but for the people's, God meeting their anticipated unbelief with visible proof, condescending to weak faith.

themes: the LORD God of their fathers
a sign for the people's faith

schools.summary: The second sign of the leprous hand — 'put thine hand into thy bosom... his hand was leprous as snow' (the hand struck with leprosy white as snow at God's word); the worst form, the 'white disease,' the skin glossy white, the hair white as wool, the law's marks of the leper (Clarke, Ellicott); the lesson — what is in fullest vigour withers in a moment at God's command, and the dead is restored, God killing and making alive (Calvin); signifying Moses should bring sore diseases on Egypt and remove them, and Israel, leprous in bondage, be cleansed (Wesley); a sign in Moses' own flesh, more startling and personal than the first.

themes: put thine hand into thy bosom

schools.summary: The hand restored — 'put thine hand into thy bosom again... it was turned again as his other flesh' (the leprous hand, put back, drawn out wholly healed, sound as the rest, Keil and Delitzsch); the cure complete, no scar or remnant, as sudden and perfect as the smiting; the leprosy healed at God's word (Gill); the double lesson — as God can in a moment strike the healthiest flesh with the most loathsome incurable disease, so in a moment he can cleanse it, both wounding and healing in his hand; leprosy beyond man's cure, so healing it instantly the clearer proof of divine power.

themes: put thine hand into thy bosom again
turned again as his other flesh

schools.summary: The voice of the signs — 'if they will not believe the first sign... they will believe the voice of the latter' (God foreseeing some yield to the first, others hold out till the second, furnishing more than one proof); 'the voice of the sign' — not Moses' voice attested by it but the voice the sign itself utters, the wonder declaring God's message, Ps 105:27 (Ellicott); God's works having a voice to be diligently observed (Wesley); the two signs meeting differing degrees of stubbornness (Clarke); God taking away every handle for doubt, heaping the measure of proof to overflowing (Calvin), condescending to human weakness.

themes: if they will not believe the first sign
the voice of the latter sign

schools.summary: The third sign of water to blood — 'if they will not believe also these two signs... the water... shall become blood upon the dry land' (Nile water poured on the ground turning to blood); the Hebrew repeating the verb for emphasis, 'shall assuredly become blood' (Ellicott); the signs chosen partly for ease of performance before the people, yet significant — this third pointing forward to the first great plague, striking at the revered Nile; God heaping up his proofs, three signs in ascending force meeting three degrees of doubt, leaving the obstinate without excuse (Calvin, Gill).

Moses' objection of weak speech — 'I am not eloquent... slow of speech, and of a slow tongue' (literally 'not a man of words'; words not coming readily, speaking with difficulty, formerly and now, Clarke/Keil); the reluctance no longer excused as humility but betraying cowardice, slothfulness, and unbelief (Henry), Moses catching at every word of escape, his distrust making him timid (Calvin); a great philosopher and statesman, yet no fluent speaker, thinking himself unfit before Pharaoh (Wesley); setting his own weakness against God's call, as if his lack disqualified the command, looking at himself rather than the God who sends him.

themes: slow of speech, and of a slow tongue

schools.summary: God's answer from his creative power — 'Who hath made man's mouth? or who maketh the dumb, or deaf... have not I the LORD?' (the mouth and its faculty God's own making, he appointing to one a ready tongue, to another dumbness, all human powers and defects from him); the God who made the mouth can supply its want and fit a slow tongue (Keil and Delitzsch); he who formed the organs can give utterance and take the gift away, so Moses must not provoke him (Clarke); why his hesitation was blameworthy — arrested by his own infirmity, he failed to look up to God, above the want of human aid (Calvin).

The promise of divine help in speaking — 'Now therefore go' (the objection answered, Moses bidden simply to obey); the twofold promise 'I will be with thy mouth, and teach thee what thou shalt say' (God undertaking both to assist his utterance and supply the very words, to suggest the matter and aid the speaking, Ellicott); the parallel with the promise to the disciples that it would be given them what to speak, the Spirit in them (Mt 10:19), and God's word to reluctant Jeremiah (Jer 1:6); God putting words in his mouth, so the slow tongue no hindrance when God is with it (Gill); the sufficiency in God, not Moses.

themes: I will be with thy mouth
teach thee what thou shalt say

schools.summary: Moses' final reluctance — 'send, I pray thee, by the hand of him whom thou wilt send' (a request that someone else be sent in his place); a curt, impatient, scarcely reverent speech, meaning God would do better to send another (Ellicott); the kindling of God's anger (v14) confirming it was no holy prayer but a faithless shrinking, Moses out of particular objections now begging in general to be excused; even the meek may, pressed by fear, resist a clear call.

debates.question: Does Moses here pray for the mission of the Messiah?

sides.position: no — it is a curt, impatient plea to be excused, that God send some other person; the kindling of God's anger (v14) shows it was faithless reluctance, not a pious prayer
some ancient fathers (Justin, Tertullian, Cyprian, Eusebius) took it as a prayer that God send the promised Messiah rather than Moses
holders: the patristic reading noted by Clarke and Calvin

themes: send by the hand of him whom thou wilt send

schools.summary: God's anger and the appointing of Aaron — 'the anger of the LORD was kindled against Moses' (the only such expression in the call; his repeated objections run from modesty into unbelief); even self-diffidence, when extreme and hindering duty, offensive to God (Henry, Wesley); no better sacrifice than to obey God's voice, 1 Sam 15:22 (Calvin); the anger confirming his words were faithless reluctance, not a prayer for the Messiah (Clarke); yet mercy mingled — 'Is not Aaron the Levite thy brother? I know that he can speak well' (the eloquent brother appointed spokesman); 'he cometh forth to meet thee... glad in his heart' (God moving Aaron toward him, a glad reception assured).

themes: the anger of the LORD was kindled

schools.summary: The joint commission — 'thou shalt speak unto him, and put words in his mouth' (Moses receiving God's message and conveying it to Aaron, furnishing the matter; Aaron, eloquent, clothing it in language and speaking it fluently); 'I will be with thy mouth, and with his mouth' (God assisting both the giving and the speaking, suggesting the matter to Moses, the language to Aaron, Ellicott); even gifted Aaron could not speak to purpose unless God were with his mouth, the best gifts failing without grace (Wesley); 'teach you what ye shall do' (God directing their action too); Moses the source, Aaron the voice, God the power behind both.

themes: with thy mouth, and with his mouth
teach you what ye shall do

schools.summary: The relation of Moses and Aaron — 'he shall be thy spokesman unto the people' (Aaron declaring to Israel and Pharaoh the message Moses receives); 'instead of a mouth' (Aaron serving as Moses' mouth, voicing what Moses gives, as lips utter the mind's thoughts); 'thou shalt be to him instead of God' (Moses in God's place to Aaron, God speaking to Moses and Moses to Aaron, not addressing Aaron directly, Aaron recognizing in Moses God's authority and revelation, Ellicott); the language dignifying Moses, not degrading him — made a god to Aaron, the source of the divine word; God conferring honour while supplying Aaron, not displacing him (Calvin); foreshadowing the prophet's office.

The rod of authority — 'take this rod in thine hand, wherewith thou shalt do signs' (the same staff changed into a serpent, v3, now the instrument for the wonders before Pharaoh and Israel); God choosing so humble a thing on purpose, to confound the pride of Pharaoh, the mighty works wrought by a despised instrument and the glory plainly God's (Calvin); the very staff Moses carried as a shepherd, that he need not be ashamed of his mean condition, now his staff of authority instead of sword and sceptre, the badge of God's power through him (Wesley); becoming 'the rod of God' (Ex 4:20), carried through all the Exodus wonders.

themes: take this rod in thine hand

schools.summary: Moses taking leave of Jethro — 'Let me go... and return unto my brethren in Egypt' (Moses, kindly received and long Jethro's shepherd, not departing without asking leave, civil to his benefactor and just to his service, Henry); the close tie — by marriage to Zipporah, Moses received into the tribe, making the asking fitting (Ellicott); a reserve Calvin finds surprising — Moses speaking only of human feeling, the desire to see his brethren, not mentioning the divine vision and commission; the prudence in the silence, stating a modest sufficient reason; Jethro's gracious 'Go in peace.'

themes: let me go and return unto my brethren

schools.summary: The renewed command and removal of danger — 'Go, return into Egypt' (a second word urging present execution of the commission, the peremptory tone a spur, Moses perhaps delaying after Jethro's leave, Ellicott); 'all the men are dead which sought thy life' (the danger that drove Moses from Egypt forty years before now past, those who sought to kill him for slaying the Egyptian dead, including not only Pharaoh of Ex 2:23 but the murdered man's kindred who might pursue a blood-feud); God tenderly removing the human fear that might hold Moses back, clearing the path, meeting not only his objections but his unspoken fears.

The departure with family and rod — 'took his wife and his sons, and set them upon an ass' (Zipporah and his two sons, Gershom and lately-born Eliezer, Ex 2:22); taking his whole household showing he meant to dwell in Egypt and believed God would work the deliverance (Gill, Calvin); the single ass a plain proof how humble his condition and slender his substance, the man called to confront the king setting out in poverty (Calvin); 'took the rod of God in his hand' (the shepherd's staff now named 'the rod of God,' the instrument of his mighty works, Wesley); the weakness of the man and the power of the rod side by side.

themes: took his wife and his sons
the rod of God in his hand

schools.summary: The wonders and the hardening — 'see that thou do all those wonders before Pharaoh... which I have put in thine hand' (not chiefly the three signs for Israel but the great portents and plagues for Pharaoh, appointed in God's counsel and committed to Moses); 'I will harden his heart, that he shall not let the people go' (forewarning that the wonders will be met by obstinate refusal); God disclosing the hard outcome beforehand, that Moses not be discouraged when the king resists.

debates.question: In what sense does God 'harden' Pharaoh's heart?

sides.position: judicially and permissively — God hardens only after Pharaoh repeatedly hardens himself against the light, at last permitting Satan to harden it effectually; Pharaoh's guilt is his own
as the execution of God's sovereign decree, ordaining to display his power in Pharaoh's resistance

themes: do all those wonders before Pharaoh
he shall not let the people go

schools.summary: Israel named God's firstborn son — 'Thus saith the LORD' (the first time this prophetic preface is put into a man's mouth, Moses speaking as God's ambassador, Wesley); 'Israel is my son, even my firstborn' (God claiming the nation as his own son, the dearest and most honoured); the Hebrew people unutterably dear to God (Clarke); the tender relation now first revealed no mere metaphor but a reality — the sonship of adoption, flowering in the NT and Hosea 11:1 'out of Egypt have I called my son,' Israel taken into the True Son (Ellicott); refuting Pharaoh's pretext for refusal, God's firstborn having the prior claim (Calvin).

The demand and threat — 'Let my son go, that he may serve me' (God requiring the release of his firstborn that Israel may worship, which they could not do in Egypt, sacrifice being the chief part of their worship and the animals sacred to Egypt, Clarke); 'if thou refuse... I will slay thy son, even thy firstborn' (the divine sonship of Israel answered by the threatened death of Pharaoh's heir in just retribution); the threat not delivered at the outset but the final clause, pressed when all else failed, just before the tenth plague, Ex 11:5 (Calvin, Ellicott); as Pharaoh deals with God's people, so shall he be dealt with — firstborn for firstborn (Wesley).

themes: Let my son go, that he may serve me

schools.summary: The dark encounter at the inn — 'by the way in the inn' (not an inn as we mean but a recognized resting-place, little shelter, Ellicott); 'the LORD met him, and sought to kill him' ('met' in a bad sense, an adverse, hostile encounter, God's hand against Moses to interrupt his journey, Calvin; the LXX softening to 'the angel of the Lord,' acting by an angel of death or sudden deadly sickness); the cause (from the next verses) Moses' neglect to circumcise his son, the covenant sign, arising perhaps from being unequally yoked with an indulgent Midianite wife (Wesley); the man sent to deliver the covenant people having failed to keep the covenant in his own house; the passage obscure yet improving (Henry).

debates.question: Whom did the LORD seek to kill, and why?

sides.position: Moses himself, for neglecting to circumcise his son — the covenant sign slighted in the house of the one sent to deliver the covenant people (some hold the threat fell on the uncircumcised son)

themes: by the way in the inn

schools.summary: Zipporah's act of circumcision — perceiving the neglect was the cause of the displeasure, and Moses too ill to act, she 'took a sharp stone, and cut off the foreskin of her son' (Gill); the stone knife anciently used for circumcision, reckoned purer than metal, Josh 5:2 (Ellicott); the wife improperly assuming an office not hers, seizing the stone hastily under fear (Calvin); the obscure gesture 'cast it at his feet' (the foreskin laid at Moses' feet, or touched to them); 'Surely a bloody husband art thou to me' (Zipporah, a Midianite, distressed and reproachful at the bloody rite, calling Moses a husband of blood whose life she had as it were redeemed by her child's blood); the covenant sign restored, the wrath turned aside.

themes: cut off the foreskin of her son

schools.summary: The danger removed — 'so he let him go' (God, or the angel, or the deadly sickness, sparing Moses' life; God allowing him to recover, accepting Zipporah's act, late though it was, as sufficient reparation, Ellicott; the disease and trembling departing the moment the child was circumcised, Gill); 'A bloody husband thou art, because of the circumcision' (Zipporah repeating her cry in explanation, the verse appending her reason); Moses likely sending Zipporah and the children back to Jethro for a time (Wesley); God jealous for his covenant even in his servant, but the breach repaired, restoring favour — obedience averting the wrath disobedience kindled.

The meeting of the brothers — 'the LORD said to Aaron, Go into the wilderness to meet Moses' (God, who told Moses Aaron was coming, v14, now moving Aaron, by some secret powerful working on his mind or an angel's ministry, so exactly that they met at the appointed place, Clarke); 'in the mount of God' (Horeb, the Sinaitic region of the burning bush, where Aaron would not have known to come without divine guidance, Ellicott); 'and kissed him' (the affectionate greeting of brothers long parted, Aaron supposing him perhaps dead in exile, now overjoyed to find him alive and chosen, Calvin); the providence ordering the meeting precisely; God's promise of a glad reception made good.

themes: Go into the wilderness to meet Moses
met him in the mount of God

schools.summary: Moses informing Aaron — 'Moses told Aaron all the words of the LORD... and all the signs' (holding nothing back, declaring both the message and the wonders, the signs being the three miracles of vv3-9, Ellicott); the reason — since Aaron was to be Moses' mouth and spokesman, opening the commission to Israel and Pharaoh, he must know fully what God said and was to be done (Gill); the lesson — fellow-servants of God in the same work should use mutual freedom and rightly understand one another (Wesley); Moses the recipient of revelation faithfully passing it to Aaron, the partnership of vv15-16 put into practice.

themes: told Aaron all the words of the LORD

schools.summary: The gathering of the elders — 'Moses and Aaron went and gathered together' (the two brothers, united in the commission, setting about it at once, having returned together, Ellicott); 'all the elders of the children of Israel' (the heads and representatives of the people); the practical reason — the whole multitude, grown to a great nation, could not be assembled in one place, so the elders were called as representatives to receive the message and convey it (Calvin, Gill); the orderly course, beginning with the recognized leaders; fulfilling God's instruction to bring the message to Israel before going to Pharaoh (Ex 3:16).

Aaron's speaking and the signs done — 'Aaron spake all the words which the LORD had spoken unto Moses' (the appointed spokesman declaring the whole message Moses received and conveyed, Aaron as Moses' mouth, fulfilling the command of v16, Gill/Ellicott); Aaron, having dwelt in Egypt, likely the more fluent in Hebrew and fitter to address the people (Clarke); 'did the signs in the sight of the people' (the three wonders given to Moses — rod to serpent, the leprous and healed hand — performed before the elders to confirm the message); the arrangement defined at the mount put into action, the message both spoken and visibly authenticated.

themes: in the sight of the people

schools.summary: The people's belief and worship — 'the people believed' (the very thing Moses feared would not happen, v1, come to pass; Israel crediting the divine appointment of Moses and Aaron, the signs confirming the testimony, Clarke; believing Moses sent of God to deliver them, Gill); 'that the LORD had visited the children of Israel, and looked upon their affliction' (God at last regarding his people in bondage, remembering his covenant, the long-awaited visitation begun); 'they bowed their heads and worshipped' (overcome with the news of deliverance, bowing in thankful adoration); perhaps a part for the whole, or all with one consent embracing the message (Calvin); the chapter opening with Moses' fear of unbelief, closing with the people's faith.
